column25:

column26:
Instruction compliance for exploratory experimental tasks (2nd-4th conditions) 
[NB: Participants who fail to follow instructions for these exploratory tasks (e.g., are too shy to sing solo for the alternating singing condition) will still be included in the main confirmatory analysis.] - Instruction compliance
column27: Do you have any comments about this experiment?

This group is shy and sings in low volume

I clearly mentioned to all participants not to interact before directed, however I noticed some of them interacted anyway (whispering to their peers). Unfortunately, it is quite 'normal' behaviour because they were afraid of making mistakes. So they feel they need to clarify with peers anyway. I did mention that they could ask me any questions anytime. 
I gave 40 for the compliance of the first task. I noticed during the conversation, they did not start speaking even when they are given time to. I think that happened because they were very shy to start first. They only started talking maybe on 1 last minute out of 2.5 minute time they had. So not everyone had a chance to talk.

I gave 80 for the compliance of other tasks.
The next task which was the individual singing. I noticed they gained more confidence but some started to sing together. 
When the tasks were about doing activities together, they can do them very well.

Reflecting from this, I realized that this behaviour is quite normal. Growing up I noticed that people did not want to start first in discussion, passive in class, and were afraid of becoming the spotlight (afraid of making mistakes). 

When the participants were competing the last part of the survey, there was a blackout. However, I kept observing them and they stayed focus on completing it. When they finished, they left the room one by one.

I gave 30 for the compliance on the first task because I could see that they understand the direction but they were super hesitant to start. They looked nervous and a few of them even made mistakes in recitating. For the other tasks I gave them 80 because they became more confidence in working on it. 
I feel like their response reflects people in their surroundings in general. That's why I gave the particular scores.

I gave 50 for the first task because even though they were hesitant, they managed to finally start and did the task quite well. 
For the rest of tasks, I gave them 70, because they did well in the individual singing and group recitation. However during the group conversation, the participant on the rightmost side took thirty seconds to answer, thus not everyone got a chance to speak/discuss.

The video camera in the experiment room crashed during the karaoke session, but we still have the audio recording from the experiment room microphone to verify task compliance. In the middle of the turn-taking singing task, Zoom of the experiment room computer crashed and became the main window seen by the participants (they lost the video of the experiment) and assumed it was part of the procedure. After waiting a bit, one participant restored the video to the main window of the experiment room computer and they continued the session (though they had lost the turn-taking task in the meantime). When we entered the room and realized they had missed the turn-taking part, we re-ran that portion of the experiment. As a result, they completed the final Qualtrics questionnaire before performing the turn-taking singing task. All other parts of the experiment were completed in the correct order.
In the alternating singing condition, the participant on the other side of the video started, but they were corrected immediately and then the condition proceeded in the correct direction.

During the singing condition, there was a signal issue that caused the karaoke video shared from a different computer to buffer in a way that was out of tempo. This also meant that some transition from verse to verse were omitted by the group, because they had no singer good enough to catch it.

One person (participant ID 5) spoke Slovak in the conversation instead of Czech. This is a mutually intelligible language, and encountering it occasionally in conversation in Prague is not rare, so it did not affect the rest of the conversation or do, but for MV3 this participant probably needs to be excluded because the singing and speaking is not in the same language.

This was a merry group, they were having a good time. There was talk between participants before the start, and during the experiment. At one point at the beginning they started calling for help - just because one participant couldn't submit the Qualtrics page. I had to look inside and make sure everyone is ok. The qualtrics issue resolved itself, but all of it was discussed by everyone in the room. People were making jokes, etc. And that attitude continued throughout the experiment, people discussed not their replies but what they were doing, what was not working, their attitude to the tasks, kept making jokes.
Apart from that, I think they complied

This group was mostly compliant. There was one woman who struggled with Qualtrics, she lost her tab (maybe accidentally closed it) after the first condition, didn't know how to get back to it. She left the room, I explained to her that the QR code is attached to the table at the front. She went back inside, scanned the QR code and started the survey again. I think during the second condition (recitation), she was speaking the text and filling out the survey at the same time.
I understand that two other participants misunderstood the instructions about the survey and filled out everything, I don't know at which point. I was surprised to see them walking out almost instantly after the last condition. I know they submitted, they showed me the "100%" screen. When I listened back to the recording, I heard them saying that they had filled it out too early. I don't know exactly who that was, we can find out on the video if needed.

Here compliance was nearly perfect. There were two couples among the group, one of them was chatting when they arrived, but stopped when they were reminded not to. The couples didn't sit together in the lab.
This week went quite smoothly. Participants complied with experimental protocol well!

One of the participants also tapped the mic during the session (just so you know for the noise). Overall, the experiment went well, though there seemed to be conversation happening while waiting for the different instructions.

The internet connection slowed down during the group singing, so from time to time the group and the accompaniment did not match. Thankfully, all group members continued singing until the very end of the song.

We had a technical problem - the monitor cable started flickering, and we had to replace it during the experiment. As a result, we switched off and started another Zoom meeting to continue from the same point. The break lasted no more than 3–4 minutes, and the participants waited calmly while we fixed the problem.

Despite the fact that we strictly forbade participants (via email, a note on the door, and personal conversation) from communicating with one another, all three experiments included moments in which brief question–response exchanges occurred outside the instructions. It appears that for highly social Georgian participants, the very nature of the experiment - particularly its involvement with music and communication - was perceived as amusing and enjoyable, making it difficult for them to resist occasional verbal interaction.

Mostly fine. In the alternating singing condition they finished too early and didn't loop around to start again from the top. This resulted in a little speaking. Other conditions seemed to be fine though.

In the group chanting condition, they skipped all the verses and only chanted the chorus. This resulted in some laughter around half-way through. During debriefing there was some confusion amongst the participants about why this happened, but possibly a misinterpretation of the slides by one person, and others just followed. An interesting moment of collective decision-making! 

Other conditions seemed to be fine.

Otherwise it all went well.

It was very difficult for the participants to use the online survey, due to familiarity with the process and also due to internet problems, The zoom recording stopped at some point (before condition 3) due to the same reasons, It was also impossible to keep participants away from interacting with each other during the completion of the first part of the survey, maybe because it was too long but also perhaps because some participants needed support from their pairs. I also needed to intervine to assist with the instructions, but I always moved apart during the experiments. I hope this was enough for the validity of the data.

It was very difficult for some of the participants to use the online survey, due to familiarity with the process and also due to internet problems. It was also impossible to keep participants away from interacting with each other during the completion of the first part of the survey, maybe because it was too long but also perhaps because some participants needed support from their pairs. I also needed to intervine to assist with the instructions, but I always moved apart during the experiments. I hope this was enough for the validity of the data. The session was recorded by an external camera as well as by zoom. Note that in this condition, the line by line singing task was previous to the collective signing task, this was a bit confusing because the video with the lirics and the melody was attached to the collective signing task. In general the group worked well.
This was an interesting task to do and a learning curve... I can't wait to see how the analysis goes.
The group was singing much better than the first one...I'm happy

Two participants came out in the beginning (when filling out the first part of the qualtrics survey) asking where they can find their participant ID. I pointed them to the number written on the informed consent.

The participant that was to start the conversation started before the countdown, so before the 2.5 minutes (he also did not talk about the question initially but I think how he liked the song), but the group continued discussing until the 2.5 minutes were over. The same happened for the turn-taking singing condition. One participant came late (but within the initial 10 minute countdown), he reported that he had filled out the survey in time, thus before the group singing started.

This last group had more challenges in dealing with the survey, so we needed to assist some of them. The Internet connection was particularly unstable, due to the rain, but we managed to go through the whole experiment. Despite the problems with the survey, the attitude of the cohort was very possitive towards the experiment.
A fair number of participants showed up than I had expected and their participation was impressive, which makes this a fair group for expected data analysis.
